status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: AI-powered insurance claims processing with damage photo analysis, fraud detection, automated liability assessment, and settlement recommendation engine.

plan.description: Define business requirements, user stories, and process flows for the insurance claims lifecycle — intake, photo upload, fraud scoring, liability assessment, and settlement recommendation.
acceptance_criteria: All actor roles documented with their goals and pain points
End-to-end claims lifecycle mapped with decision points
Acceptance thresholds defined for fraud score, damage estimate, and settlement amount
Edge cases documented: total loss, disputed liability, multi-party claims

plan.description: Produce the product requirements document (PRD), define success metrics (claims cycle time, fraud catch rate, settlement accuracy), and prioritize the MVP feature set.
acceptance_criteria: PRD approved with in-scope and out-of-scope features explicitly listed
Quantitative success metrics defined with baseline and target values
MVP scope locked: damage analysis + fraud flag + settlement recommendation
RACI matrix defined for human-approval gates

plan.description: Produce threat model, STRIDE analysis, penetration test plan, and SBOM for the insurance claims platform. Address PII (claimant data), PCI DSS (payment for settlements), and SOC 2 controls.
acceptance_criteria: All data flows classified: PII, PCI, internal
STRIDE threats enumerated per component with mitigations assigned
SBOM generated covering all runtime dependencies
Pen test plan covers authentication, upload injection, payment bypass, model inversion
PCI DSS SAQ completed
Encryption at rest and in transit

plan.description: Produce compliance artifacts for PCI DSS, SOC 2, and SOX: risk matrix, traceability matrix, V&V protocol templates, and audit evidence checklist.
acceptance_criteria: Every functional requirement linked to at least one compliance control
Risk matrix covers likelihood × impact for all identified threats
V&V protocol includes test IDs that map back to requirements
Audit evidence checklist approved by compliance officer

plan.description: Design wireframes and interaction flows for: claimant self-service portal (photo upload, status tracking), adjuster workbench (AI recommendations, override workflow), and fraud analyst dashboard.
acceptance_criteria: All primary user journeys wireframed end-to-end
AI recommendation cards show confidence scores and explainability
Human override UI clearly distinguishes AI suggestion from final decision
Accessibility audit passes WCAG 2.1 AA for all primary flows

plan.description: Design and implement the database schema: claims, claimants, policies, photos, damage_assessments, fraud_scores, liability_assessments, settlements, audit_events. Includes migrations and seed data.
acceptance_criteria: All PII columns encrypted at rest with column-level encryption
Foreign key constraints enforce referential integrity
Audit_events table captures every state transition with actor, timestamp, reason
Migrations are idempotent and reversible
Seed data covers 50+ representative claim scenarios including fraud cases
Transaction isolation level documented
Audit trail on all writes

plan.description: Build data pipelines for: ingesting historical claims data, labeling damage severity from photos, computing fraud feature vectors, and feeding the analytics schema for reporting.
acceptance_criteria: ETL pipeline processes 10k historical claims with < 5% error rate
Photo feature vectors extracted and stored in feature store within 30s per image
Fraud feature pipeline produces ≥ 25 engineered features per claim
dbt models tested with schema tests and data freshness assertions
Analytics mart refreshes on daily schedule with SLA alerting

plan.description: Train and evaluate the damage photo analysis model (computer vision — severity classification and repair cost estimation) using transfer learning on vehicle/property damage datasets.

payload.training_data: historical claims photos with adjuster-verified labels

acceptance_criteria: Damage severity classification F1 ≥ 0.82 on held-out test set
Cost estimate within ±15% of adjuster-verified amount on 80% of cases
Model card documents training data, known biases, and performance across claim types
Grad-CAM heatmaps generated per inference for explainability
Model versioned in MLflow with reproducible training run
Bias evaluation performed
Model card generated

plan.description: Train and evaluate the fraud detection model using claim metadata, behavioral signals, network graph features, and historical fraud labels. Output: fraud probability score + top contributing features.
acceptance_criteria: Fraud detection AUC-ROC ≥ 0.88 on held-out labeled dataset
False positive rate ≤ 8% at operating threshold to protect legitimate claimants
Fairness audit shows no statistically significant performance disparity across protected groups
Top contributing features surfaced per prediction for adjuster review
Model card includes limitations and recommended human review triggers
Bias evaluation performed
Model card generated

plan.description: Build the liability assessment engine (rules + ML hybrid): determines fault percentage per party using police reports, photos, telematics, and jurisdiction rules. Build the settlement recommendation engine using damage estimate + liability + policy limits.

payload.liability_engine: rules-based jurisdiction layer + ML override for ambiguous cases
payload.settlement_engine: regression model over damage_cost × liability_pct × policy_coverage

acceptance_criteria: Liability assessment matches adjuster final decision within 10% on 75% of test cases
Settlement recommendation within ±10% of final settled amount on 70% of cases
Jurisdiction rules loaded from YAML and easily extensible without code changes
Confidence intervals calibrated (ECE ≤ 0.05) on validation set
Bias evaluation performed
Model card generated

plan.description: Configure the multi-agent coordinator pattern for the claims processing pipeline. The coordinator routes each claim through: photo-analysis agent → fraud-detection agent → liability-assessment agent → settlement-recommendation agent with HITL gates.
acceptance_criteria: Coordinator routes claims to all four specialist agents in correct dependency order
HITL gate triggers on fraud_score > 0.6, settlement > $25k, or total_loss classification
Agent outputs include trace_id for full audit chain
Config files allow threshold adjustment without code changes

plan.description: Build the core FastAPI backend: claims CRUD, photo upload (S3 presigned URLs), async ML inference pipeline, proposal/approval store, settlement payment initiation, and webhook handlers.

endpoints: POST /approvals/{id}/decision — approve or reject AI proposal
payload.storage: S3 for photos, PostgreSQL for structured data

acceptance_criteria: All endpoints return typed Pydantic response models
Photo upload uses presigned S3 URLs — backend never streams file bytes
Async ML pipeline triggered on photo upload, result stored on completion
All state transitions written to audit_events table atomically
API returns 400 with structured error body for all validation failures
Authentication via JWT with role-based access (claimant, adjuster, fraud_analyst, admin)

plan.description: Produce the OpenAPI specification for the insurance claims API, including request/response schemas, authentication requirements, error codes, and webhook contracts.

payload.auth: Bearer JWT + API key for service-to-service

acceptance_criteria: All endpoints documented with example requests and responses
Error codes enumerated: 400 validation, 401 auth, 403 forbidden, 404 not found, 422 unprocessable, 500 internal
Webhook payloads documented with retry and signing specifications
Postman collection passes automated schema validation
OpenAPI spec passes spectral linting with zero errors

plan.description: Build the claimant self-service portal: claim submission wizard, drag-and-drop photo upload with preview, real-time processing status, AI assessment display, and settlement offer acceptance flow.
acceptance_criteria: Claim submission wizard completes in ≤ 5 steps with progress indicator
Photo upload supports drag-and-drop, multiple files, shows upload progress
AI assessment card shows damage severity, cost estimate, and confidence score
Settlement offer card clearly distinguishes AI recommendation from final offer
All interactive elements meet WCAG 2.1 AA contrast and keyboard navigation requirements

plan.description: Build the adjuster workbench: claims queue with priority sorting, AI recommendation panel with explainability (heatmaps, top features), override workflow, fraud investigation view, and batch settlement approval.

features: real-time claim queue updates via SSE

acceptance_criteria: Adjuster can review and decision a claim in ≤ 3 clicks from queue view
Damage heatmap overlaid on photo with severity annotation
Fraud score shows top 3 contributing features with plain-language explanations
Override requires mandatory reason selection from predefined taxonomy
Audit trail shows all AI proposals, human decisions, and timestamps in chronological order

plan.description: Build the fraud analyst dashboard: network graph visualization of related claims/claimants/repair shops, fraud ring detection alerts, model performance metrics, and case management workflow.
acceptance_criteria: Network graph renders up to 500 nodes with pan/zoom without performance degradation
Fraud ring alerts group related claims with shared entities highlighted
Model metrics dashboard shows live AUC, precision, recall, and false positive rate
Case escalation form captures all required fields for SIU referral

plan.description: Set up Docker Compose for local development and Kubernetes manifests for production: API server, Celery workers, ML inference service, PostgreSQL, Redis, S3-compatible storage, Airflow, and MLflow.
acceptance_criteria: `docker-compose up` starts all services and API health check passes within 60 seconds
Helm charts parameterized for staging and production environments
ML inference service scales independently from the API service
Secrets managed via environment variables — no hardcoded credentials in any config file
Resource limits defined for all containers

plan.description: Implement CI/CD pipelines: automated testing, Docker image build and push, ML model validation gate, staging deployment, and production promotion with rollback capability.

payload.model_gate: fail pipeline if new model AUC drops > 2% from baseline

acceptance_criteria: CI pipeline runs on every PR and must pass before merge
ML model validation gate blocks deployment if performance degrades beyond threshold
Production deployment requires manual approval step in GitHub Actions
Rollback to previous version executable via single pipeline trigger
Pipeline completes in ≤ 15 minutes for standard code changes

plan.description: Write unit tests for all backend services, ML model inference wrappers, fraud feature engineering pipeline, settlement calculation engine, and HITL approval workflow.
acceptance_criteria: Test suite passes with 0 failures on clean checkout
Line coverage ≥ 85% on all backend modules
ML inference tests use fixture inputs and assert output schema + value ranges
Settlement calculation tests cover edge cases: $0 liability, policy cap exceeded, multi-party split
HITL workflow tests verify proposals are created and require approval before execution

plan.description: Write integration tests for the end-to-end claims pipeline: photo upload → ML analysis → fraud scoring → liability assessment → settlement recommendation → adjuster approval → settlement execution.

payload.framework: pytest with testcontainers for PostgreSQL and Redis

acceptance_criteria: All 6 scenario paths pass end-to-end against real database and queue
Fraud flag scenario confirms HITL gate fires and blocks auto-settlement
Adjuster override correctly replaces AI recommendation in audit log
Each test cleans up its own test data on completion

plan.description: Execute system-level performance and load tests: simulate 500 concurrent claim submissions, ML pipeline throughput under load, and settlement payment processing latency.
acceptance_criteria: API p95 latency ≤ 500ms at 500 concurrent users
ML inference p95 ≤ 3000ms for photo analysis under load
Error rate ≤ 0.1% under peak load scenario
System recovers to baseline performance within 60 seconds after load spike ends
Performance baseline JSON committed for regression comparison in CI

plan.description: Execute security validation: OWASP Top 10 scan on the API, dependency vulnerability scan (SBOM), authentication bypass testing, file upload security testing, and payment flow security review.
acceptance_criteria: Zero critical or high OWASP ZAP findings in production build
No CVE score ≥ 7.0 in runtime SBOM without documented waiver
SQL injection attempts return 400 with no stack trace leakage
IDOR test confirms claimants cannot access other claimants' claims
Payment amount tampering blocked server-side — amount always recomputed from DB
PCI DSS SAQ completed
Encryption at rest and in transit

plan.description: Generate final compliance evidence package: completed traceability matrix linking requirements to tests, SOC 2 control evidence, PCI DSS SAQ, and SOX ITGC test results. Prepare audit-ready artifact bundle.
acceptance_criteria: Every SOC 2 Trust Services Criterion mapped to at least one implemented control with evidence
PCI DSS SAQ-D completed with all applicable requirements marked compliant or N/A with justification
SOX ITGC covers: logical access, change management, computer operations, data backup
Traceability matrix shows 100% requirement coverage by passing tests
Audit bundle reviewed and signed off by compliance officer

plan.description: Write operational runbooks: incident response for ML model degradation, fraud system outage, payment processing failure, and PII data breach. Define SLAs and on-call escalation paths.
acceptance_criteria: Each runbook includes: detection criteria, immediate actions, escalation path, and post-incident review steps
SLA definitions include measurement methodology and consequence of breach
Monitoring dashboards cover: API latency, ML model drift, fraud flag volume, settlement queue depth
PII breach runbook aligns with regulatory notification timelines (72h GDPR, applicable state laws)

plan.description: Produce technical and user-facing documentation: API reference (auto-generated from OpenAPI), architecture decision records (ADRs), ML model cards, and adjuster user guide.
acceptance_criteria: API reference generated from OpenAPI spec and renders correctly in docs site
Each ADR documents: context, decision, alternatives considered, and consequences
All three model cards include: intended use, training data description, performance metrics, known limitations, and fairness considerations
Adjuster user guide covers all primary workflows with screenshots
README includes: local setup in ≤ 10 commands, architecture diagram, and environment variable reference

plan.description: Develop training materials for adjusters, fraud analysts, and compliance officers covering: how to interpret AI recommendations, when to override, how to use the HITL approval queue, and model limitations.
acceptance_criteria: Training materials cover all three audience roles with role-specific content
Override procedures documented with real examples of legitimate override reasons
Model limitations section clearly states what the AI cannot reliably assess
Quick reference card fits on one printed page per role
Training materials reviewed and approved by product and compliance teams

flaws: Training data for the vision model (Step 8) assumes 'historical claims photos with adjuster-verified labels' as a given — but acquiring labeled insurance photo datasets is a multi-month legal and procurement effort. No carrier will hand over raw claims photos. There is no data acquisition, licensing, or data sharing agreement step anywhere in this plan. Without training data, Steps 8–10 and everything downstream cannot execute.

GNN for fraud detection (Step 9) has a severe cold-start problem. A graph neural network requires a dense, labeled historical fraud graph to produce useful embeddings. For a new system with no transaction history, you have no edges, no fraud labels at scale, and no graph structure. This is a research-grade deliverable disguised as an MVP task. It will not hit AUC-ROC ≥ 0.88 without 2–3 years of historical claims data with confirmed fraud labels.

Automated settlement recommendations are legally regulated at the state level in the US. Most state insurance codes require a licensed adjuster (a human) to issue the final settlement determination. Deploying a 'settlement recommendation engine' without state insurance department review and potentially prior approval creates regulatory liability that could result in operating license revocation. No regulatory pre-approval step exists in this plan.

PCI DSS compliance for 'settlement payment initiation' (Step 12) is vastly underspecified. The plan lists 'POST /claims/{id}/settle' as an endpoint but names no payment rail (ACH, check, wire transfer), no payment processor integration (Stripe, Plaid, bank API), and no scoping of PCI DSS cardholder data environment. PCI DSS SAQ-D without a defined payment architecture is checkbox theater.

Column-level AES-256 encryption (Step 6) has no key management implementation. pgcrypto or application-level encryption requires a KMS (AWS KMS, HashiCorp Vault, GCP KMS). Without KMS, encryption keys are stored adjacent to the data they protect — defeating the control. No KMS selection, integration, or key rotation policy is anywhere in this plan.

The EXIF metadata tension is unresolved: Step 9 uses 'damage_photo_metadata_anomalies' as a fraud signal (GPS, device model, timestamp) but PII regulations (CCPA, GDPR, and carrier data minimization policies) typically require stripping GPS coordinates from uploaded photos. You cannot simultaneously use EXIF GPS as a fraud feature and comply with data minimization. This architectural conflict is never acknowledged.

No model monitoring or drift detection is implemented. Steps 8–10 produce models; Step 24 mentions 'ML model drift' in runbooks — but there is no implementation step for production model monitoring (data drift, prediction drift, PSI scores, shadow scoring). MLflow is listed only for training. A fraud model that silently degrades in production is a compliance failure and a financial loss event.

The critical path is dangerously long: Steps 1→6→7→8+9→10→11→12 must complete sequentially before any end-to-end integration test can run. This means 10 steps of unbounded research and ML work (data pipelines, model training, feature engineering) before you can validate a single claim flows through the system. If Step 7's ETL produces poor features, Steps 8–10 must be retrained — there is no feedback loop or parallel prototype path.

Appeals processing has no backend. Step 5 wireframes an appeals flow; Step 14 builds an AppealsForm component — but no backend endpoint, state machine, or database table handles the appeals lifecycle. The UI will submit to nothing.

Step 7's photo feature extraction SLA (30 seconds per image) is incompatible with Step 21's load test of 500 concurrent submissions. At 500 concurrent claims each with multiple photos, you need GPU-backed inference infrastructure. Step 17's infrastructure spec lists no GPU nodes, no GPU-optimized instance types, and no autoscaling policy for the ML inference service. The SLA will be violated under any realistic load.

Fraud model feature importance output (top 3 contributing features surfaced to adjusters) leaks model internals. Bad actors can iteratively probe which features trigger lower fraud scores and tune claim submissions accordingly — a model gaming attack. No adversarial robustness or feature obfuscation strategy is mentioned.

Celery task idempotency is unaddressed. If a worker crashes mid-analysis (after photo analysis but before fraud scoring), the claim is in an inconsistent intermediate state. No idempotency keys, no at-least-once/exactly-once semantics, and no compensation logic are specified. Under load this will produce claims stuck in 'analyzing' forever.
suggestions: Add a Step 0: Data Strategy. Define the training data source explicitly — licensed datasets (CCC ONE, Mitchell, Tractable APIs), synthetic data generation, or a pilot data-sharing agreement with a carrier. Without this, Steps 7–10 have no foundation. Timebox this as a go/no-go gate before committing to ML model development.

Replace GNN fraud detection in MVP with a well-tuned gradient boosted model (XGBoost/LightGBM) on tabular features. Ship the GNN as a Phase 2 enhancement once you have 12+ months of production fraud labels. The acceptance criteria of AUC-ROC ≥ 0.88 is achievable with tabular models on good features; it is not achievable with a GNN on sparse cold-start data.

Add a dedicated Legal/Regulatory Review step (after Step 2, before Step 3) that specifically covers: state insurance department notification/approval requirements, whether automated settlement outputs require a licensed adjuster signature, adverse action notice obligations under FCRA for fraud-flagged claimants, and ECOA/disparate impact analysis for the fraud model.

Add KMS integration to Step 6 as a hard requirement: specify the KMS provider, key hierarchy (master key → data encryption keys), key rotation schedule, and envelope encryption pattern. This is a prerequisite for PCI DSS and SOC 2 compliance, not a nice-to-have.

Make the photo pipeline decision explicit: either strip EXIF GPS on upload (privacy-first) and use only device model/timestamp for fraud features, or retain GPS under explicit consent with documented legal basis. Document this as an ADR. Do not allow this ambiguity to reach production.

Add a Model Monitoring step (between Steps 18 and 19): implement Evidently AI or WhyLogs for production drift detection, configure alerting when PSI > 0.2 on key fraud features, and define the retraining trigger criteria. The runbook in Step 24 cannot reference a capability that was never built.

Introduce a vertical slice integration test after Step 12: before building Steps 13–16 (frontend), wire a single happy-path claim through the stub ML pipeline with hardcoded model responses. This validates the full backend data flow, catches schema mismatches early, and reduces the risk of discovering fundamental architecture issues after 3 frontend sprints.

Specify a payment gateway in Step 12 (Stripe, Dwolla for ACH, or a bank API). Define whether settlements are check, ACH, or wire. PCI DSS scope changes dramatically based on this choice — a tokenized gateway approach can reduce PCI scope to SAQ-A rather than SAQ-D, which is a multi-month compliance effort difference.

Add rate limiting and abuse prevention to Step 12's acceptance criteria: per-IP limits on photo upload endpoints, per-claimant claim submission rate limits, and signed S3 presigned URL expiry (5 minutes max). These are not stretch goals — they are table stakes for a public-facing upload endpoint.

Add a named feature store technology to Step 7 (Feast, Hopsworks, or even a simple Redis + PostgreSQL pattern) and include feature serving latency as an acceptance criterion. 'Feature store schema' as a markdown document is not a feature store.
missing: Training data acquisition strategy — the single largest gap. The entire ML pipeline (Steps 7–10) has no defined data source.
State insurance regulatory pre-approval process for AI-assisted claims and automated settlement recommendations.
KMS/secrets management implementation step — encryption is specified everywhere but the key management infrastructure is never built.
Model monitoring and drift detection implementation — mentioned in runbooks but not built.
Payment gateway integration and payment rail selection.
Disaster recovery / backup-restore implementation with defined RTO/RPO.
GPU infrastructure specification for ML inference at load.
Adversarial robustness testing for the damage vision model (adversarial image attacks are a known vector in insurance fraud).
Appeals processing backend (state machine, endpoints, database tables).
Legal review of FCRA adverse action notice obligations when a claimant is fraud-flagged and denied or delayed settlement.
GDPR Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) — any EU claimant triggers this requirement for automated decision-making under GDPR Article 22.
Rollback strategy for ML models in production (not just code rollback — model rollback with data migration).
Feature store technology selection and serving-latency SLA.
security_risks: S3 presigned URL expiry is unspecified. Long-lived presigned URLs (>1 hour) shared in emails or browser history become persistent access vectors to PII-containing claim photos. Specify max 5-minute expiry with one-time-use enforcement.

Fraud model feature importance output enables model gaming. Publishing 'top 3 contributing features' per claim to adjusters means those features are one social engineering call away from being known to organized fraud rings. Consider surrogate explanations that describe behavior without revealing exact feature weights.

ML model inversion attacks: the damage severity classifier and cost estimator are queryable via API. A sophisticated attacker can reconstruct approximate training data or discover decision boundaries by making many inference requests. No rate limiting or input validation on the inference endpoint is specified.

JWT algorithm confusion attack is listed in Step 22's test cases but no mitigation is specified in Step 12's implementation. Explicitly pin the algorithm to RS256 in the JWT validation middleware — do not accept 'alg: none' or HS256 with a public key.
Webhook handlers (Step 12) are vulnerable to SSRF if the webhook URL is user-controlled. Validate all webhook destinations against an allowlist and block RFC 1918 ranges.

Celery task serialization: if using pickle serializer (Celery default), a malicious Redis compromise enables arbitrary code execution via deserialized task payloads. Enforce JSON serialization in Celery configuration.

EXIF GPS data in uploaded photos constitutes precise location PII. If stored unstripped in S3, a breach exposes claimant home/work locations. Ensure EXIF stripping occurs server-side before storage, not client-side where it can be bypassed.

Settlement amount recomputation (Step 22 acceptance criteria correctly flags 'amount always recomputed from DB') must also apply to liability percentage and policy limits — not just the settlement dollar amount. A partial tampering attack could manipulate liability_pct via a race condition if these values are passed through client state.
final_review.summary: This is a technically coherent plan with well-structured HITL gates, reasonable compliance surface awareness, and appropriate ML acceptance criteria — but it has three fatal gaps that prevent production deployment in a regulated insurance context. First, there is no training data acquisition strategy: the vision model and fraud model both assume labeled historical data that does not exist without carrier partnerships or licensed dataset procurement, making the entire ML stack speculative. Second, automated settlement recommendations in insurance are state-regulated — deploying this without insurance department review in each operating jurisdiction is a license-revocation risk, and this regulatory approval process is entirely absent from the plan. Third, key management for the encryption controls is never implemented — encryption is mandated at every layer but the KMS infrastructure that makes encryption meaningful is missing. Beyond these blockers, the GNN fraud model is a cold-start impossibility for an MVP, model drift monitoring is referenced in runbooks but never built, and payment rail selection is deferred to the point where PCI DSS scoping is undefined. For an MVP score calibration: the core backend architecture and HITL patterns are solid (would score ~68 in isolation), but the regulatory and data gaps are not polish items — they are architectural prerequisites that invalidate the shipping timeline as written. Fundamental rework required before this can be considered production-ready.

Fraud model feature importance output enables model gaming. Publishing 'top 3 contributing features' per claim to adjusters means those features are one social engineering call away from being known to organized fraud rings. Consider surrogate explanations that describe behavior without revealing exact feature weights.

ML model inversion attacks: the damage severity classifier and cost estimator are queryable via API. A sophisticated attacker can reconstruct approximate training data or discover decision boundaries by making many inference requests. No rate limiting or input validation on the inference endpoint is specified.

JWT algorithm confusion attack is listed in Step 22's test cases but no mitigation is specified in Step 12's implementation. Explicitly pin the algorithm to RS256 in the JWT validation middleware — do not accept 'alg: none' or HS256 with a public key.
Webhook handlers (Step 12) are vulnerable to SSRF if the webhook URL is user-controlled. Validate all webhook destinations against an allowlist and block RFC 1918 ranges.

Celery task serialization: if using pickle serializer (Celery default), a malicious Redis compromise enables arbitrary code execution via deserialized task payloads. Enforce JSON serialization in Celery configuration.

EXIF GPS data in uploaded photos constitutes precise location PII. If stored unstripped in S3, a breach exposes claimant home/work locations. Ensure EXIF stripping occurs server-side before storage, not client-side where it can be bypassed.

Settlement amount recomputation (Step 22 acceptance criteria correctly flags 'amount always recomputed from DB') must also apply to liability percentage and policy limits — not just the settlement dollar amount. A partial tampering attack could manipulate liability_pct via a race condition if these values are passed through client state.
history.summary: This is a technically coherent plan with well-structured HITL gates, reasonable compliance surface awareness, and appropriate ML acceptance criteria — but it has three fatal gaps that prevent production deployment in a regulated insurance context. First, there is no training data acquisition strategy: the vision model and fraud model both assume labeled historical data that does not exist without carrier partnerships or licensed dataset procurement, making the entire ML stack speculative. Second, automated settlement recommendations in insurance are state-regulated — deploying this without insurance department review in each operating jurisdiction is a license-revocation risk, and this regulatory approval process is entirely absent from the plan. Third, key management for the encryption controls is never implemented — encryption is mandated at every layer but the KMS infrastructure that makes encryption meaningful is missing. Beyond these blockers, the GNN fraud model is a cold-start impossibility for an MVP, model drift monitoring is referenced in runbooks but never built, and payment rail selection is deferred to the point where PCI DSS scoping is undefined. For an MVP score calibration: the core backend architecture and HITL patterns are solid (would score ~68 in isolation), but the regulatory and data gaps are not polish items — they are architectural prerequisites that invalidate the shipping timeline as written. Fundamental rework required before this can be considered production-ready.
